executiveSummary:
Today in ML, three threads pull on the same knot: interpretability is converging on attention-head-level analysis, a disagreement is emerging about whether heads are the right unit at all, and two papers offer competing ablation strategies.

Both papers analyze circuits at the attention-head level, using different ablation strategies but reaching compatible conclusions about head specialization. Smith argues that head-level analysis is sufficient for understanding task-specific reasoning; Chen counters that activation patches capture more of the causal structure.

If you read one paper today, make it 2504.01234 — its mechanistic account of multi-step reasoning circuits directly addresses your work on interpretability.

themes.title: Interpretability converges on attention heads
themes.argument: Both papers analyze circuits at the attention-head level, using different ablation strategies but reaching compatible conclusions about head specialization. Smith argues attention-head-level analysis is sufficient; Chen counters that activation patches are more reliable — a tension worth watching.

papers.title: Circuit-level analysis of reasoning
papers.onelinePitch: A mechanistic account of how attention heads compose into multi-step reasoning circuits.
papers.whyMatters: Directly grounded in your stated interest in mechanistic interpretability.

papers.title: Head pruning ablations
papers.onelinePitch: Ablation evidence that only a small subset of attention heads matter for task-specific reasoning.
papers.whyMatters: Tests the framing from your March 3 starred paper on circuit sparsity.
